title: How to handle relationships when you are changing and they are not

htmlContent:
The Loneliest Intersection

You wake up one day and the ground under your feet feels different. You've been doing the work. Therapy, maybe. Reading. Sitting with your own discomfort instead of running from it. You've started to notice your patterns, the ones that kept you small, the ones that kept you safe in ways that weren't actually safe anymore.

And then you look at the people around you. Your partner. Your closest friend. Your sibling. And you realize they haven't moved an inch.

Here's the thing. This is one of the most painful parts of healing. You're not the same person you were six months ago, or a year ago, or three years ago. But the people who knew the old version of you, they're still expecting that person to show up. And when you don't, they get confused. Or scared. Or angry. Or all three.

I've been there. I know how it feels to be proud of the changes you've made, the boundaries you've finally set, the self-awareness you've fought for, and then have someone look at you like you've betrayed them. Like your growth is an attack on them.

It's lonely. It's disorienting. And it makes you question everything. Am I being unreasonable? Am I the one who changed for the worse? Should I just go back to the way things were so everyone feels comfortable again?

Stay with me here. Because the answer to that last question is no. You shouldn't go back. But that doesn't make the path forward any less complicated.

What the Science Says About Change and Relationships

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, would tell you that your nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and threat. This is called neuroception. It happens below conscious awareness. When you change, even in positive ways, your nervous system is sending out different signals than it used to. And the people around you, their nervous systems pick up on that shift.

If they're used to you being the quiet one, the people-pleaser, the one who never says no, and suddenly you're standing in your truth, their nervous systems register that as unfamiliar. And unfamiliar often gets tagged as unsafe. Not because you're doing anything wrong. But because their systems are wired for predictability.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. When you start to heal your own trauma, you're literally changing the way your body holds tension, how you breathe, how you respond to stress. That's not just an internal change. It's an external one too. People can feel it. They might not have the words for it, but they sense that something is different.

And here's where it gets tricky. The people who are not changing, they're still operating from their old survival strategies. If their strategy was to keep you in a certain role so they could feel stable, your growth threatens that stability. They might push back. They might guilt you. They might say things like "You're not the person I fell in love with" or "You think you're so much better than everyone now."

Right? You've heard those words. Or something like them.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work walks you through exactly what happens when you start to outgrow the survival patterns that kept you alive but also kept you stuck. And he doesn't sugarcoat how relationships change in the process.

The Grief of Outgrowing People

Let's call this what it is. It's grief. You're grieving the relationship you thought you had. You're grieving the version of yourself that was easier for them to handle. You're grieving the fantasy that everyone could just grow together at the same pace.

I remember sitting across from a friend I'd known for fifteen years. We used to bond over our shared trauma. We'd complain about our families, our jobs, our inability to say no. It was a connection built on mutual struggle. And when I started to heal, when I started to set boundaries and stop complaining as much, she looked at me and said, "You've changed. I don't feel like I can talk to you anymore."

That sentence cut deep. Because she was right. I had changed. And she couldn't talk to me the same way. Not because I was judging her. But because I was no longer willing to stay in the pit with her. I was climbing out. And she wasn't ready to climb with me.

Think about it. When you change, you're not just changing yourself. You're changing the entire dynamic of every relationship you're in. The dance steps are different now. And your partner, your friend, your family member, they have to learn new steps too. Some of them will try. Some of them won't.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). Lindsay Gibson's work helped me understand that my patterns of over-functioning and caretaking were not my fault. They were adaptations. And when I stopped over-functioning, the people who benefited from my over-functioning got very uncomfortable.

That's the part nobody tells you about healing. The people who are comfortable with your old patterns will resist your new ones. Not because they're bad people. But because your change forces them to look at themselves. And a lot of people would rather stay in denial than face their own pain.

What You Can Actually Do (And What You Can't)

So what do you do when you're changing and they're not? First, you need to get clear on what you can control and what you can't. You can control your own boundaries. You can control how much you explain yourself. You can control whether you show up to the same old arguments and fall into the same old traps.

You cannot control whether they change. You cannot control how they feel about your growth. You cannot control whether they choose to do their own work.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). Nedra Glover Tawwab's approach is straightforward. She doesn't make boundaries sound mystical or complicated. She just tells you what they are and how to hold them. And when you're in a relationship where the other person isn't changing, boundaries become your lifeline.

Here's what I mean. You can't make your partner go to therapy. But you can say, "I'm not going to participate in arguments where I'm being blamed for my growth. I'll talk to you when you're ready to speak respectfully." You can't force your friend to examine their own trauma. But you can say, "I love you, and I can't be the only one doing the emotional labor in this friendship."

Notice what just happened in your body when you read those examples. Did your chest tighten? Did your stomach clench? That's the fear of abandonment. That's the old survival system screaming, "Don't say that! They'll leave you!" And they might. Some people will leave when you set boundaries. That's the risk.

But here's the thing I had to learn the hard way. The people who leave because you set a healthy boundary were never really there for you anyway. They were there for the version of you that didn't take up space. They were there for the version of you that absorbed their discomfort. That's not love. That's a transaction.

Wild, right? You spend your whole life trying to be acceptable so people will stay. And then you realize that being acceptable meant being a ghost in your own life.

Navigating the In-Between

You don't have to cut everyone off. That's a common fear, that healing means becoming isolated. For some people, that is the path, at least temporarily. But for many of us, it's about learning to hold the tension of being in relationship with people who are in a different place than we are.

This is what Janina Fisher, who specializes in trauma-informed therapy, calls "the internal family systems approach." You have parts of you that want to grow and parts that want to stay safe by keeping the peace. Both are valid. Both need to be heard.

So you might have a part that wants to confront your parent about the past. And another part that's terrified of losing the only family you have. Neither part is wrong. Your job is to hold both with compassion and make decisions from a place of grounded adulthood, not from a reactive child part or a rebellious teenager part.

I've found that the relationships that survive this transition are the ones where there's at least some willingness on both sides to be uncomfortable. You're uncomfortable because you're changing. They're uncomfortable because your change is asking them to change too. Can you both sit in that discomfort without blaming each other?

Sometimes the answer is yes. I've had friends who initially resisted my growth, then months later came back and said, "I see what you're doing now. I'm starting to do my own work." That's beautiful when it happens. But it doesn't always happen.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). Deb Dana's work on Polyvagal Theory gives you concrete tools for regulating your nervous system when you're in those uncomfortable relational moments. Because let's be honest, when someone you love is pushing back against your growth, your system is going to light up. You need tools to stay grounded.

Sit with that. You are not bad for changing. You are not wrong for outgrowing people. You are not selfish for wanting relationships that reflect who you are now, not who you were.

What Happens When You Stay True

I can't promise you that everyone will come along. I can't promise you that the relationships that matter most to you will survive your transformation. That's the hard truth. Some won't. And that will break your heart.

But I can tell you this. The relationships that do survive will be deeper, more honest, and more alive than anything you had before. Because you're not hiding anymore. You're not performing. You're not shrinking yourself to fit into someone else's comfort zone.

And the new relationships you'll attract, the ones that come after you've done the work, they'll be built on something real. You won't have to pretend to be someone you're not. You won't have to apologize for your needs. You won't have to explain why boundaries matter.

So here's what I want you to take from this. Keep changing. Keep growing. Keep doing the work even when it makes the people around you uncomfortable. Your healing is not a betrayal of anyone. It is a return to yourself.

And the people who are meant to be in your life will adjust. Not because you forced them. But because your grounded, honest, boundary-holding presence will eventually become familiar to their nervous systems too. It takes time. It takes patience. It takes a lot of deep breaths.

But you're worth it. You've always been worth it.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) - Nedra Glover Tawwab
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - Lindsay Gibson
  • Anchored (paid link) - Deb Dana
